sections.content: Overview

Large language models have fundamentally transformed how we approach natural language understanding and generation, but their raw output often requires sophisticated post-processing and decoding strategies to produce coherent, contextually appropriate, and controllable responses. The challenge extends beyond simply training massive neural networks—it involves carefully selecting which tokens to generate at each timestep, managing the probability distributions over vocabularies containing tens of thousands of items, and implementing strategies that prevent mode collapse, hallucinations, and semantically incoherent sequences. Without proper decoding strategies such as beam search, temperature scaling, top-k sampling, and nucleus sampling, even well-trained models produce repetitive loops, inconsistent narratives, and factually impossible outputs. Furthermore, inference-time optimization through techniques like speculative decoding and cached key-value pairs becomes critical for production systems handling millions of queries daily, where latency directly impacts user experience and operational costs. This lesson explores the sophisticated mechanisms that transform raw probability distributions into human-quality text, the mathematical foundations underlying temperature and probability constraints, and the engineering patterns that enable LLMs to operate at scale in real-world applications.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider Virat Kohli facing a fast bowler in a Test match during the powerplay. At each delivery, Kohli must decide whether to play a defensive stroke (low-risk, modest runs), an aggressive drive (high-risk, potentially high reward), or leave the ball entirely (zero risk but zero runs). The bowler's delivery speed, line, and length create a probability distribution of possible outcomes—some shots are more likely to be successful than others based on field placement and batting conditions. Just as Kohli's decision-making process at each ball determines the match's trajectory, an LLM's decoding strategy at each token determines whether the generated text flows naturally or becomes incoherent. Temperature in LLM decoding works like Kohli's risk tolerance: at low temperature (like a defensive Test innings), the model picks the highest-probability token almost always, producing conservative, repetitive output; at high temperature (like an aggressive T20 innings), the model explores lower-probability alternatives, creating variety but risking nonsensical sequences. Beam search resembles keeping multiple promising batting strategies in mind simultaneously—if the aggressive approach gets you out, you pivot to the conservative strategy you've been tracking in parallel, ensuring you don't get trapped by a single bad decision early in the innings. Understanding this parallel reveals why decoding strategy is not an afterthought but a fundamental design choice that controls the model's behavior just as Kohli's mindset determines his approach to each delivery—both require balancing risk, consistency, and strategic objectives.
Core Concepts
Temperature Scaling and Probability Distribution Control

Temperature is a hyperparameter that controls the sharpness of the probability distribution over the vocabulary when sampling the next token in an autoregressive language model. Mathematically, the temperature-scaled softmax is computed as P(token_i | context) = exp(logit_i / T) / Σ_j exp(logit_j / T), where T is temperature and logits are the raw neural network outputs before normalization. When T approaches 0, the distribution becomes increasingly peaked around the token with the highest logit—the model becomes deterministic and always selects the argmax token, producing repetitive and conservative text. Conversely, when T is large (typically > 1), the softmax flattens the distribution, making all tokens nearly equiprobable regardless of their logit values, which increases diversity but sacrifices coherence and consistency. At T = 1 (the default), the softmax uses unmodified logits. Temperature directly controls the entropy of the sampling distribution: lower temperatures reduce entropy and exploration, while higher temperatures increase it. In production systems, temperature is often exposed as a user-facing parameter allowing control over creativity versus consistency, with typical ranges being 0.1 to 2.0 depending on the application domain.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Imagine Jasprit Bumrah deciding his bowling strategy for an over against a specific batter. With low temperature (say 0.3), Bumrah commits entirely to his most reliable delivery—the yorker—because it has the highest success probability against this batter; he repeats it almost every ball, creating a predictable but effective pattern. With moderate temperature (1.0), Bumrah maintains his full arsenal proportional to expected success rates: mostly yorkers, but occasionally mixing in bouncers and slower balls based on their actual success probabilities; this reflects the natural variation in his bowling decisions. With high temperature (2.0), Bumrah essentially ignores his success probabilities and bowls wildly varied deliveries—sometimes wide full tosses, sometimes bouncing off a length—creating unpredictability but risking poor results because he's ignoring what actually works against this batter. The logits in the neural network represent Bumrah's assessment of each delivery type's effectiveness (their 'success scores'), and temperature determines whether he follows those assessments rigidly (low T) or treats them loosely (high T). Understanding this reveals why temperature is so critical: it's not just mathematical notation but a fundamental dial controlling whether the model acts as a rigorous decision-maker (low T, like a cautious Test cricket bowler) or an exploratory improviser (high T, like an experimental T20 bowler), and most real-world applications need this dial perfectly tuned to their specific use case.
Top-K and Nucleus (Top-P) Sampling

Top-k sampling and nucleus sampling (top-p) are truncation strategies that address a fundamental problem with naive temperature scaling: flattening the distribution improves diversity but can make low-probability tokens—including nonsensical or toxic ones—too likely to be selected. Top-k sampling restricts the sampling pool to only the k tokens with the highest probabilities, then renormalizes the distribution over just those tokens. For example, with k=50, after sorting tokens by probability and selecting the top 50, the model samples exclusively from those 50 tokens, completely ignoring the remaining 50,000 tokens in the vocabulary. Nucleus sampling (also called top-p) takes a different approach: instead of fixing k, it sorts tokens by probability and selects the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds a threshold p (typically 0.9). This is adaptive—if the distribution is very peaked (one token dominates), nucleus sampling might select only 5 tokens; if the distribution is flat, it might select 500 tokens. The key advantage of nucleus sampling over top-k is that it respects the model's inherent confidence: when the model is highly certain about the next token, nucleus sampling preserves that certainty by selecting fewer tokens, but when the model is uncertain, it allows more exploration. Both strategies eliminate the probability of sampling from the long tail of extremely low-probability tokens, which often correspond to spelling errors, nonsensical words, or context breaks, thereby significantly improving output quality without sacrificing all diversity.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider the field placement decisions of a captain like Pat Cummins setting a field for a bowler attacking a specific batter. With no sampling strategy (naive), the captain considers all 11 fielding positions equally probable based on theoretical benefits—but some positions are clearly worse than others and are practically never used. With top-k=5 sampling, Cummins decides 'I will only consider my 5 most effective field placements against this batter' (say, slips, cover, mid-wicket, square leg, and fine leg), completely ignoring impractical positions like fielders behind the bowler's arm at leg slip when there's no left-hander advantage. With nucleus (top-p=0.9) sampling, Cummins adapts dynamically: against an aggressive batter with multiple scoring zones, he might activate 8-10 field placements because the captain is genuinely uncertain where the batter will score; against a cautious batter with one clear scoring weakness, Cummins might commit to just 3-4 tight placements because he's highly confident about where runs will come. The probability distribution represents the captain's assessment of each field placement's defensive value, and both strategies ensure the captain focuses on genuinely useful options while completely eliminating impractical positions that would paradoxically become active if we just flatten the probability distribution. This parallel reveals that sampling constraints aren't arbitrary restrictions but principled ways to honor the model's actual confidence while preventing it from being seduced into nonsense by the long tail of the distribution.
Beam Search and Constrained Decoding

Beam search is a structured decoding algorithm that explores multiple hypothesis sequences in parallel, maintaining the top-b most likely sequences at each timestep (where b is the beam width). Unlike greedy decoding, which commits to the highest-probability token at each step (a myopic strategy that often produces suboptimal results), beam search defers commitment and keeps multiple promising paths alive. At each timestep t, the algorithm expands each of the b surviving hypotheses by generating probabilities for all vocabulary tokens, computes the joint log-probability of each (b × |vocab|) possible continuation, and then prunes back to the top b based on cumulative probability. The key insight is that this non-greedy exploration can overcome local optima: sometimes selecting a token with 75% probability leads to dead ends, while a lower-probability token with 60% probability opens doors to much higher overall sequence probability. Beam search requires sophisticated bookkeeping to track hypothesis genealogy, manage memory for b different sequences simultaneously, and implement stopping criteria (typically when hypotheses reach end-of-sequence tokens). Constrained decoding extends these ideas by forbidding certain tokens, enforcing format constraints (like JSON structure), or guiding the model toward specific topics through dynamic vocabulary restrictions. Techniques like guided beam search reweight probabilities during decoding to favor tokens that move toward desired outcomes. While beam search produces higher-quality results than greedy or sampling strategies on many tasks, it comes with computational costs: decoding time scales roughly linearly with beam width, and the improved quality must be weighed against latency constraints in real-time applications.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Imagine Rohit Sharma and his team planning a Test cricket innings with strategic depth. Using greedy decoding (the myopic approach), Rohit commits to his initial strategy—'I'll attack aggressively'—and plays accordingly for the first 10 overs; but by over 12, he realizes the ball is seaming heavily and his aggressive approach is backfiring, yet he's already cemented his reputation and can't easily reset. With beam search (b=3), Rohit maintains three simultaneous strategic hypotheses: aggressive attacking, cautious grinding, and aggressive-but-selective, playing shots that keep all three strategies viable as long as possible; if the aggressive approach starts failing by over 12, he pivots to one of the other hypotheses he's been maintaining, having never fully committed. The cumulative log-probability represents innings score projections: Rohit's team expands the top 3 most promising innings strategies at each boundary or dismissal decision point, computes expected final scores for each possible next decision, and always maintains the top 3 strategies. Constrained decoding is like adding captain's instructions: 'You must score at 3 runs per over minimum' or 'You cannot play square of the wicket until lunch' or 'You must use the powerplay aggressively'—these constraints eliminate certain token choices and guide Rohit toward outcomes that satisfy external requirements. Understanding this parallel reveals beam search's true power: it's not just exploring more paths but maintaining multiple strategic theories simultaneously and pivoting gracefully when evidence suggests the current path is suboptimal—a principle that applies equally to LLM decoding and Test cricket strategy.
Sequence Length and Stopping Criteria

Sequence length control and stopping criteria determine when an autoregressive language model terminates token generation, a problem that seems simple but introduces substantial complexity in production systems. The primary stopping signal is generation of an end-of-sequence (EOS) token, which the model learns to emit when it has completed a logical unit (a response, a sentence, a code block). However, models often fail to emit EOS tokens reliably—they may continue generating indefinitely, producing repetitive loops, degraded quality, or running out of context window. Thus, production systems implement multiple stopping criteria: maximum length limits (e.g., 'stop after 512 tokens'), token count budgets based on input prompt length and model context window, explicit stop sequences ('if you generate <|stop|> or three newlines, terminate'), and length penalties that gradually reduce the model's preference for continuing as the sequence grows. Length penalties work by dividing the token's logit by (sequence_length)^alpha, where alpha controls the strength of the penalty; this encourages shorter outputs without hard limits. Another critical consideration is token counting and context window management: GPT-3.5 has a 4096-token context window, GPT-4 has up to 128K tokens, and different tokenizers (BPE variants, SentencePiece) count the same text differently, creating off-by-one errors in production. Beam search complications arise because different hypotheses have different lengths, requiring careful handling of length normalization when comparing sequences of unequal lengths to prevent systematic bias toward shorter outputs.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider a Test match innings where the batter (the model) must decide when to declare. The natural stopping point is when the batter's team has achieved their strategic objective (like scoring 400 runs to build a commanding lead), analogous to the model generating an EOS token indicating a complete thought. However, batters sometimes don't recognize when to stop—they keep batting beyond the point of strategic benefit, and their team must intervene with hard rules: 'You can only bat for 90 overs maximum' (context window limits), 'If you're still batting after lunch on day 4, we're declaring' (maximum length limits), or 'Once you score 350, we'll start considering declaration' (length penalties). The captain (the inference engine) monitors the innings carefully: if the batter is on 320 runs with only 10 overs left in the playing session, the captain knows declaration is imminent, so running singles becomes less valuable than attempting boundaries (low-probability tokens become less valuable as EOS token probability rises). Different batting partnerships produce innings of different lengths—one might naturally reach 250 and declare, another 450, and the captain must adjust field placement strategy accordingly (managing beam search with sequences of different lengths). If the captain had only 400 balls to bowl (context window), they must count carefully whether the batter has used 398 or 402 balls, as miscounting leads to running out of balls mid-match (token budget overflow). The stopping criteria parallel reveals that knowing when to stop is as crucial as knowing what to generate—both require combining model signals, hard constraints, and strategic context to achieve optimal outcomes.

import numpy as np
from typing import List, Tuple, Dict
import math

class CricketLLMDecoder:
    """Advanced LLM decoding strategies for cricket commentary generation."""
    
    def __init__(self
sections.vocab_size:
int = 10000):
        self.vocab_size = vocab_size
        # Simulated logits for demonstration
        self.logits = np.random.randn(vocab_size)
        
        # Cricket-themed token mappings
        self.token_to_word = {
            0: "<EOS>",
            1: "Virat",
            2: "Kohli",
            3: "boundary",
            4: "wicket",
            5: "bowling",
            6: "Bumrah",
            7: "innings",
        }
    
    def temperature_scaled_softmax(self
sections.temperature:
float) -> np.ndarray:
        """Apply temperature scaling to control probability distribution."""
        # Subtract max for numerical stability
        scaled_logits = logits / temperature
        shifted = scaled_logits - np.max(scaled_logits)
        exp_logits = np.exp(shifted)
        probs = exp_logits / np.sum(exp_logits)
        return probs
    
    def top_k_sampling(self

sections.k:
int) -> int:
        """Sample from top-k tokens only."""
        # Apply temperature
        probs = self.temperature_scaled_softmax(logits, temperature)
        
        # Get top-k indices
        top_k_indices = np.argsort(probs)[-k:]
        top_k_probs = probs[top_k_indices]
        
        # Renormalize
        top_k_probs = top_k_probs / np.sum(top_k_probs)
        
        # Sample from top-k distribution
        selected_idx = np.random.choice(top_k_indices, p=top_k_probs)
        return selected_idx
    
    def nucleus_sampling(self

sections.p:
float = 0.9) -> int:
        """Nucleus (top-p) sampling for adaptive vocabulary constraint."""
        # Apply temperature
        probs = self.temperature_scaled_softmax(logits, temperature)
        
        # Sort probabilities in descending order
        sorted_indices = np.argsort(probs)[::-1]
        sorted_probs = probs[sorted_indices]
        
        # Find minimum set of tokens with cumulative probability >= p
        cumsum = np.cumsum(sorted_probs)
        nucleus_mask = cumsum <= p
        nucleus_mask[np.argmax(nucleus_mask == False)] = True  # Include first token exceeding p
        
        # Renormalize nucleus
        nucleus_probs = sorted_probs[nucleus_mask]
        nucleus_probs = nucleus_probs / np.sum(nucleus_probs)
        nucleus_indices = sorted_indices[nucleus_mask]
        
        # Sample from nucleus
        selected_idx = np.random.choice(nucleus_indices, p=nucleus_probs)
        return selected_idx
    
    def beam_search(self
sections.beam_width:
int = 3) -> List[Tuple[List[int], float]]:
        """Beam search to maintain multiple hypotheses and explore non-greedily."""
        # Initialize with empty sequence and zero log probability
        hypotheses = [([0], 0.0)]  # (token_sequence, cumulative_log_prob)
        
        for timestep in range(max_length):
            all_candidates = []
            
            # Expand each hypothesis
            for seq, log_prob in hypotheses:
                # Simulate getting logits for next token (in practice, from neural network)
                current_logits = self.logits.copy()
                probs = self.temperature_scaled_softmax(current_logits, temperature=1.0)
                
                # Generate top vocabulary options
                top_vocab_indices = np.argsort(probs)[-50:]  # Top 50 tokens
                
                for token_idx in top_vocab_indices:
                    new_seq = seq + [token_idx]
                    # Compute joint log probability
                    new_log_prob = log_prob + np.log(probs[token_idx] + 1e-10)
                    all_candidates.append((new_seq, new_log_prob))
            
            # Prune to top beam_width hypotheses
            all_candidates.sort(key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
            hypotheses = all_candidates[:beam_width]
            
            # Check for EOS tokens in top hypothesis
            if hypotheses[0][0][-1] == 0:  # EOS token
                break
        
        return hypotheses
    
    def generate_with_length_penalty(self

sections.length_penalty_alpha:
float = 0.6) -> Tuple[List[int], float]:
        """Generate sequence with length normalization penalty."""
        sequence = [1]  # Start with token 1 (Virat)
        cumulative_log_prob = 0.0
        
        for timestep in range(max_length):
            # Simulate next token logits
            current_logits = self.logits.copy()
            probs = self.temperature_scaled_softmax(current_logits, temperature=0.8)
            
            # Apply length penalty to discourage continued generation
            length_penalty = (timestep + 1) ** length_penalty_alpha
            penalized_probs = probs / length_penalty
            penalized_probs = penalized_probs / np.sum(penalized_probs)
            
            # Select token (greedy for demonstration)
            next_token = np.argmax(penalized_probs)
            sequence.append(next_token)
            cumulative_log_prob += np.log(penalized_probs[next_token] + 1e-10)
            
            # Stop if EOS or max length reached
            if next_token == 0 or len(sequence) >= max_length:
                break
        
        return sequence, cumulative_log_prob
    
    def generate_cricket_commentary(self) -> str:
        """Generate cricket commentary using nucleus sampling."""
        sequence = []
        max_length = 12  # Brief commentary
        
        for _ in range(max_length):
            current_logits = self.logits.copy()
            next_token = self.nucleus_sampling(current_logits, temperature=0.9, p=0.9)
            sequence.append(next_token)
            
            if next_token == 0:  # EOS
                break
        
        # Convert token indices to words
        commentary = [self.token_to_word.get(idx, f"token_{idx}") for idx in sequence]
        return " ".join(commentary)


# Demonstration
if __name__ == "__main__":
    decoder = CricketLLMDecoder()
    
    # Example 1: Temperature effects
    print("=== Temperature Effects ===")
    logits = np.array([5.0, 4.0, 3.0, 2.0, 1.0])
    for temp in [0.3, 1.0, 2.0]:
        probs = decoder.temperature_scaled_softmax(logits, temp)
        print(f"Temperature {temp}: {probs}")
    
    # Example 2: Beam search
    print("\n=== Beam Search Results ===")
    hypotheses = decoder.beam_search(max_length=5, beam_width=3)
    for i, (seq, log_prob) in enumerate(hypotheses):
        print(f"Hypothesis {i+1} (log_prob={log_prob:.3f}): {seq}")
    
    # Example 3: Length penalty generation
    print("\n=== Length Penalty Generation ===")
    seq, log_prob = decoder.generate_with_length_penalty(max_length=8, length_penalty_alpha=0.7)
    print(f"Generated sequence: {seq}")
    print(f"Cumulative log probability: {log_prob:.3f}")
    
    # Example 4: Cricket commentary
    print("\n=== Generated Cricket Commentary ===")
    commentary = decoder.generate_cricket_commentary()
    print(f"Commentary: {commentary}")

sections.content: The code demonstrates all four core decoding strategies working together. The CricketLLMDecoder class encapsulates temperature scaling through the `temperature_scaled_softmax` method, which divides logits by temperature before computing softmax—when temperature is low (0.3), probabilities concentrate on high-probability tokens like a cautious cricket strategy; when high (2.0), they flatten like an exploratory T20 approach. The `top_k_sampling` method restricts vocabulary to the k highest-probability tokens and renormalizes, preventing the model from selecting from the distribution's long tail of problematic tokens—analogous to a captain using only their best five fielding placements. The `nucleus_sampling` method adaptively selects tokens until cumulative probability reaches p (0.9), respecting the model's inherent confidence signal: when logits are extremely peaked, nucleus sampling selects few tokens; when flat, it selects more. The `beam_search` method maintains three parallel hypotheses (beam_width=3) at each timestep, expanding each by considering the top 50 vocabulary tokens, computing their cumulative log-probabilities, then pruning back to the top 3 surviving hypotheses—this non-greedy exploration prevents committing early to suboptimal paths. The `generate_with_length_penalty` method applies dynamic length normalization (sequence_length ^ 0.6) to discourage infinite generation, making later tokens progressively less likely to be selected as the sequence grows. Finally, `generate_cricket_commentary` chains these strategies together with nucleus sampling and explicit stop conditions. The demonstration shows how different temperatures produce vastly different probability distributions, how beam search explores multiple hypotheses simultaneously, and how length penalties naturally encourage reasonable sequence lengths without hard cutoffs.
How It Works Under the Hood

The internal mechanics of advanced decoding strategies involve sophisticated state management, memory optimization, and computational scheduling that differ substantially between sampling-based and search-based approaches. At the framework level (PyTorch, TensorFlow, or specialized inference engines like vLLM and TensorRT-LLM), temperature scaling requires only a single element-wise division followed by a softmax operation—computationally negligible but mathematically profound in its effects on probability distributions. Top-k and nucleus sampling require sorting the entire vocabulary (tens of thousands of elements) by probability at each timestep, which becomes a bottleneck; modern implementations use partial sorting algorithms (quickselect) to find top-k in O(vocab_size) average case rather than O(vocab_size * log(vocab_size)) full sorting. Memory management becomes critical: storing full probability distributions over 50,000+ vocabulary items for every timestep multiplies memory usage by the number of timesteps, prompting implementations to compute probabilities on-the-fly rather than storing them. Beam search introduces multiplicative complexity—maintaining b hypotheses requires b separate forward passes through the neural network, or alternatively, b-way vectorization where a single batch of b hypotheses is processed together through the transformer for efficiency. The KV-cache optimization is essential here: rather than recomputing attention key-value pairs for all previously-generated tokens, the cache stores them, reducing decoding complexity from O(sequence_length²) to O(sequence_length) per token. At inference time, speculative decoding optimizes by running a smaller draft model in parallel, generating multiple candidate tokens quickly, then validating them against the full model in a single batch forward pass—this amortizes computational cost across multiple tokens. Context window management tracks cumulative token consumption (input + generated tokens) against the model's maximum (e.g., 128K for GPT-4), with careful bookkeeping for tokenizer differences: BPE tokenizers may count 'hello' as one token or two depending on vocabulary, requiring actual tokenization rather than character-count estimates.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider the real-time mechanics of a fast-paced T20 match from the match operations center. At each ball delivery, the scoring system must update probabilities for what happens next based on batter form, bowler statistics, field placement, and match situation—this mirrors temperature scaling's runtime computation of probability distributions. The match operations team cannot store every possible prediction for all 10 million potential match situations; instead, they compute predictions on-demand and use caching: they remember that 'Rohit vs Bumrah with a slip in place' was analyzed in the last over, so they retrieve that cached analysis rather than recomputing from scratch (the KV-cache mechanism). To improve decision speed, the operations team might run a quick assistant—a junior analyst who predicts the likely next five deliveries using simplified models, then the senior analyst validates those predictions against detailed analysis, saving time overall (speculative decoding). When analyzing which field placement is optimal, the captain cannot consider all possible positions; instead, they focus the team's attention on the top-5 most promising placements and rank them (top-k sampling), or they dynamically adjust how many placements to consider based on how certain they are about the likely scoring zones (nucleus sampling). If maintaining three independent match analyses in parallel (beam search with b=3), the team uses batch processing: one analyst handles all three analyses simultaneously in parallel, consuming 3x the paper but completing in the same wall-clock time as a single analysis would take if done sequentially, because match planning has parallelizable structure. The match operations team must track their cumulative time investment carefully—if they've used 70% of their planning budget before the match reaches the innings break, they know they'll run out of time during the second innings and must pivot to faster strategies (context window management). Understanding this parallel reveals that LLM inference efficiency isn't just about algorithm selection but about intelligent resource allocation, caching, batching, and dynamic adaptation to constraints—the same principles that allow professional cricket operations centers to make split-second decisions during live matches despite the complexity involved.

import numpy as np
from collections import defaultdict
import heapq
from typing import List, Tuple, Dict, Optional

class AdvancedCricketDecoderWithOptimizations:
    """Advanced decoding with KV-cache, speculative decoding, and context tracking."""
    
    def __init__(self
sections.max_context:
int = 128000):
        self.vocab_size = vocab_size
        self.max_context = max_context
        self.kv_cache = {}  # Stores cached key-value pairs
        self.token_count = 0  # Track total token consumption
        self.cache_hits = 0
        self.cache_misses = 0
    
    class BeamHypothesis:
        """Represents a single hypothesis in beam search."""
        def __init__(self
sections.state_key:
str):
            self.token_ids = token_ids
            self.log_prob = log_prob
            self.state_key = state_key
            self.length = len(token_ids)
        
        def __lt__(self, other):
            # For heap (min-heap), use negative log prob for max-heap behavior
            return -self.log_prob < -other.log_prob
    
    def compute_attention_with_cache(self
sections.cache_key:
str) -> np.ndarray:
        """Simulate attention computation with KV-cache to avoid redundant work."""
        if cache_key in self.kv_cache:
            self.cache_hits += 1
            # Return cached computation
            return self.kv_cache[cache_key]
        else:
            self.cache_misses += 1
            # Simulate expensive attention computation
            attention_output = np.random.randn(768)  # Hidden dimension
            self.kv_cache[cache_key] = attention_output
            return attention_output
    
    def speculative_decoding(self
sections.context_tokens:
List[int], 
                            draft_model_steps: int = 3) -> Tuple[List[int], float]:
        """Use draft model for quick predictions, validate with full model."""
        generated_tokens = []
        cumulative_log_prob = 0.0
        
        # Draft model quickly generates candidate tokens
        draft_candidates = []
        for i in range(draft_model_steps):
            # Simulate draft model (faster, less accurate)
            draft_logits = np.random.randn(self.vocab_size) * 0.5
            draft_probs = self._softmax(draft_logits)
            candidate_token = np.argmax(draft_probs)
            draft_candidates.append(candidate_token)
        
        # Full model validates all draft candidates in one batch
        all_tokens = context_tokens + draft_candidates
        cache_key = "_".join(map(str, all_tokens))
        
        # Batch computation through full model
        full_logits = np.random.randn(self.vocab_size) * 1.5  # Larger variance = better model
        full_probs = self._softmax(full_logits)
        
        # Validation: accept candidates if full model agrees
        for candidate in draft_candidates:
            if full_probs[candidate] > 0.1:  # Accept if reasonable probability
                generated_tokens.append(candidate)
                cumulative_log_prob += np.log(full_probs[candidate] + 1e-10)
            else:
                # Reject: resample from full model
                next_token = np.argmax(full_probs)
                generated_tokens.append(next_token)
                cumulative_log_prob += np.log(full_probs[next_token] + 1e-10)
                break
        
        return generated_tokens, cumulative_log_prob
    
    def beam_search_with_cache(self
sections.beam_width:
int = 3) -> List[Tuple[List[int], float]]:
        """Beam search using KV-cache to optimize repeated computations."""
        # Initialize heap with starting token
        start_token = 1  # "Virat"
        start_state = "_1"
        initial_hypothesis = self.BeamHypothesis([start_token], 0.0, start_state)
        
        # Max-heap (using negative log probabilities)
        active_hypotheses = [initial_hypothesis]
        completed_hypotheses = []
        
        for timestep in range(max_length):
            candidates = []
            
            # Expand each active hypothesis
            for hyp in active_hypotheses:
                # Use cache for attention computation
                state_key = hyp.state_key
                attention = self.compute_attention_with_cache(hyp.token_ids[-1], state_key)
                
                # Generate logits (simulated)
                logits = np.random.randn(self.vocab_size) + attention[:10].mean()
                probs = self._softmax(logits)
                
                # Get top 50 tokens to avoid full vocabulary
                top_indices = np.argsort(probs)[-50:]
                
                for token_idx in top_indices:
                    new_seq = hyp.token_ids + [token_idx]
                    new_state_key = f"{hyp.state_key}_{token_idx}"
                    new_log_prob = hyp.log_prob + np.log(probs[token_idx] + 1e-10)
                    
                    new_hyp = self.BeamHypothesis(new_seq, new_log_prob, new_state_key)
                    
                    # Check for EOS token (token 0)
                    if token_idx == 0:
                        completed_hypotheses.append(new_hyp)
                    else:
                        candidates.append(new_hyp)
            
            # Keep top beam_width candidates
            candidates.sort(reverse=True)
            active_hypotheses = candidates[:beam_width]
            
            if not active_hypotheses:  # All hypotheses ended
                break
        
        # Add incomplete hypotheses
        completed_hypotheses.extend(active_hypotheses)
        completed_hypotheses.sort(reverse=True)
        
        return [(h.token_ids, h.log_prob) for h in completed_hypotheses[:beam_width]]
    
    def manage_context_budget(self
sections.output_budget:
int) -> Dict[str, int]:
        """Track context window usage and enforce stopping criteria."""
        consumed = self.token_count + input_tokens
        remaining = self.max_context - consumed
        
        return {
            "input_tokens": input_tokens,
            "previously_generated": self.token_count,
            "total_consumed": consumed,
            "remaining_budget": remaining,
            "safe_output_length": min(output_budget, max(1, remaining - 100)),  # Safety margin
            "at_risk": remaining < 1000,  # Warning if < 1K tokens left
        }
    
    def partial_sort_top_k(self
sections.k:
int) -> np.ndarray:
        """Use quickselect-style approach instead of full sort for efficiency."""
        # Simulates O(n) average case instead of O(n log n) full sort
        if k >= len(values):
            return np.argsort(values)[::-1]
        
        # Partition to find top-k efficiently
        indices = np.arange(len(values))
        # For demonstration, use heapq which is O(n log k)
        top_k_indices = heapq.nlargest(k, indices, key=lambda i: values[i])
        return np.array(top_k_indices)
    
    def _softmax(self
sections.logits:
np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """Numerically stable softmax."""
        shifted = logits - np.max(logits)
        exp_logits = np.exp(shifted)
        return exp_logits / np.sum(exp_logits)
    
    def get_cache_statistics(self) -> Dict[str, any]:
        """Return cache performance metrics."""
        total_lookups = self.cache_hits + self.cache_misses
        hit_rate = self.cache_hits / total_lookups if total_lookups > 0 else 0
        return {
            "cache_hits": self.cache_hits,
            "cache_misses": self.cache_misses,
            "total_lookups": total_lookups,
            "hit_rate": hit_rate,
            "cache_size": len(self.kv_cache),
        }


# Demonstration
if __name__ == "__main__":
    decoder = AdvancedCricketDecoderWithOptimizations()
    
    # Example 1: Context budget management
    print("=== Context Budget Management ===")
    budget = decoder.manage_context_budget(input_tokens=500, output_budget=1000)
    for key, value in budget.items():
        print(f"{key}: {value}")
    
    # Example 2: Beam search with cache optimization
    print("\n=== Beam Search with KV-Cache ===")
    hypotheses = decoder.beam_search_with_cache(max_length=8, beam_width=3)
    for i, (tokens, log_prob) in enumerate(hypotheses[:3]):
        print(f"Hypothesis {i+1} (log_prob={log_prob:.3f}): {tokens}")
    
    # Print cache statistics
    stats = decoder.get_cache_statistics()
    print(f"\nCache Stats: {stats['cache_hits']} hits, {stats['cache_misses']} misses, "
          f"hit_rate={stats['hit_rate']:.2%}")
    
    # Example 3: Speculative decoding
    print("\n=== Speculative Decoding ===")
    context = [1, 2, 3, 4]  # Example context
    draft_tokens, log_prob = decoder.speculative_decoding(context, draft_model_steps=4)
    print(f"Generated via speculation: {draft_tokens}")
    print(f"Log probability: {log_prob:.3f}")
    
    # Example 4: Efficient top-k extraction
    print("\n=== Top-K Selection Performance ===")
    logits = np.random.randn(50000)
    top_k_indices = decoder.partial_sort_top_k(logits, k=100)
    print(f"Selected {len(top_k_indices)} tokens from {len(logits)} vocabulary")
    print(f"Top-3 indices: {top_k_indices[:3]}")

sections.content: Pro Tip: In production systems serving millions of requests, KV-cache memory becomes a bottleneck rather than compute. Large-batch inference (hundreds of requests simultaneously) with shared KV-caches through prefix sharing can reduce memory overhead by 40-60%. For example, if 100 users generate responses to the prompt 'Analyze Virat Kohli's recent form', compute the prompt tokens' KV-cache once and share it across all 100 users, only diverging when generating unique output tokens. This pattern requires careful attention to cache invalidation and is implemented in systems like vLLM using PagedAttention, which applies virtual memory concepts to attention key-value storage.
Common Patterns & Best Practices

Three established patterns dominate production LLM deployments, each solving different optimization challenges. The first pattern, 'temperature + top-p sampling for diversity with safety bounds', combines moderate temperature scaling (0.7-0.9) with nucleus sampling (p=0.9-0.95) to achieve controlled diversity without catastrophic failures. This pattern avoids greedy decoding's repetitive outputs while avoiding high-temperature sampling's tendency to produce nonsensical sequences. It's computationally cheap (single forward pass per token) and is the default for most user-facing applications like ChatGPT. The second pattern, 'constrained decoding with format enforcement', applies hard constraints during generation to ensure outputs conform to JSON schemas, markdown structure, or other formats. Implementations like Guidance and Outlines reweight token probabilities during decoding to make non-compliant tokens impossible to select, transforming unconstrained generation into guided generation. This pattern is critical for applications like code generation, data extraction, or structured API responses where format violations cause downstream pipeline failures. The third pattern, 'multi-turn batching with prefix caching', optimizes multi-turn conversations by caching prompt tokens from the first user message and reusing their KV-cache for all subsequent turns, computing only the new user input and assistant response tokens in later turns. This reduces latency by 50-70% in dialogue systems compared to recomputing everything from scratch each turn.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: The first pattern (temperature + top-p) mirrors a Test cricket captain's approach during a long innings where they need to balance consistency with unpredictability. The captain (using temperature 0.8) commits mostly to the batting strategy that worked historically but occasionally deviates (nucleus sampling with p=0.9), allowing 10% of unconventional plays. This produces steady, reliable scoring (like greedy decoding) that's still occasionally surprising and engaging, preventing the match from becoming monotonously repetitive. The second pattern (constrained decoding) is like a rain-affected Duckworth-Lewis scenario where match officials have externally imposed rules: 'If Virat Kohli plays any shot that's not toward cover, the run doesn't count.' These hard constraints eliminate entire categories of decisions from being available, transforming open-ended batting strategy into guided strategy respecting external requirements. The third pattern (multi-turn prefix caching) resembles a best-of-five series where both teams play the same venue for games 2-3 after game 1; the groundcrew analyzes the pitch behavior during game 1 (computing expensive analysis once), then reuses those insights (cached KV-values) for games 2-3, only updating their analysis for new deliveries rather than completely re-examining the pitch from scratch each game. Understanding these parallel patterns reveals why they dominate production: they represent fundamental trade-offs between quality, safety, and efficiency that apply equally to LLMs and other complex systems requiring both exploration and reliability.

import numpy as np
from enum import Enum
from typing import List, Dict, Tuple

class DecodingPattern(Enum):
    GREEDY = "greedy"  # Antipattern: repetitive, boring
    TEMPERATURE_TOPK = "temperature_topk"  # Best practice: balanced diversity
    CONSTRAINED = "constrained"  # Best practice: format-guaranteed
    MULTITURNCACHED = "multiturncached"  # Best practice: efficient dialogue

class CricketCommentaryGenerator:
    """Demonstration of best practices vs antipatterns in decoding strategies."""
    
    def __init__(self
sections.19:
<ENDFORMAT>",
        }
        self.conversation_history = []  # Multi-turn tracking
        self.kv_cache = {}  # Prefix cache for multi-turn
    
    def _softmax(self
sections.logits:
np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        shifted = logits - np.max(logits)
        return np.exp(shifted) / np.sum(np.exp(shifted))
    
    # ANTIPATTERN: Greedy decoding
    def generate_greedy(self
sections.max_length:
int = 20) -> str:
        """Antipattern: Always pick the highest probability token (boring)."""
        sequence = []
        
        for _ in range(max_length):
            logits = np.random.randn(self.vocab_size) * 2
            probs = self._softmax(logits)
            
            # Always select argmax (greedy)
            next_token = np.argmax(probs)
            sequence.append(next_token)
            
            if next_token == 0:  # EOS
                break
        
        return self._decode_sequence(sequence, "Greedy (ANTIPATTERN)")
    
    # BEST PRACTICE 1: Temperature + Top-P
    def generate_temperature_topk_sampling(self

int = 20, 
                                          temperature: float = 0.8, 
                                          top_p: float = 0.9) -> str:
        """Best practice: Balanced temperature and nucleus sampling for diverse, safe output."""
        sequence = []
        
        for _ in range(max_length):
            logits = np.random.randn(self.vocab_size) * 2
            # Apply temperature
            scaled_logits = logits / temperature
            probs = self._softmax(scaled_logits)
            
            # Nucleus sampling
            sorted_indices = np.argsort(probs)[::-1]
            sorted_probs = probs[sorted_indices]
            cumsum = np.cumsum(sorted_probs)
            nucleus_mask = cumsum <= top_p
            nucleus_mask[np.argmax(nucleus_mask == False)] = True
            
            nucleus_probs = sorted_probs[nucleus_mask]
            nucleus_probs = nucleus_probs / np.sum(nucleus_probs)
            nucleus_indices = sorted_indices[nucleus_mask]
            
            next_token = np.random.choice(nucleus_indices, p=nucleus_probs)
            sequence.append(next_token)
            
            if next_token == 0:
                break
        
        return self._decode_sequence(sequence, "Temperature+TopP (BEST PRACTICE)")
    
    # BEST PRACTICE 2: Constrained decoding
    def generate_constrained_json_stats(self
sections.player:
str = "Rohit", 
                                       max_length: int = 30) -> str:
        """Best practice: Constrained generation for structured JSON output."""
        sequence = []
        state = "START"  # Track which stage of JSON we're in
        allowed_tokens = {
            "START": [17],  # <JSON> token only
            "CONTENT": list(range(1, 16)) + [0],  # Player names, stats, or EOS
            "END": [19],  # <ENDFORMAT> token
        }
        
        for step in range(max_length):
            logits = np.random.randn(self.vocab_size) * 2
            probs = self._softmax(logits)
            
            # Apply hard constraints: only allow tokens for current state
            allowed = allowed_tokens[state]
            mask = np.zeros(self.vocab_size)
            mask[allowed] = 1.0
            
            # Zero out disallowed tokens, renormalize
            constrained_probs = probs * mask
            constrained_probs = constrained_probs / np.sum(constrained_probs)
            
            next_token = np.argmax(constrained_probs)
            sequence.append(next_token)
            
            # State transitions
            if next_token == 17:  # <JSON>
                state = "CONTENT"
            elif next_token == 19:  # <ENDFORMAT>
                state = "END"
                break
            elif next_token == 0:
                break
        
        return self._decode_sequence(sequence, "Constrained JSON (BEST PRACTICE)")
    
    # BEST PRACTICE 3: Multi-turn with prefix caching
    def generate_multiturned_with_cache(self
sections.user_message:
str, 
                                       turn_number: int = 1,
                                       temperature: float = 0.8) -> str:
        """Best practice: Reuse prefix KV-cache for efficient multi-turn dialogue."""
        # Tokenize user message
        user_tokens = [hash(user_message) % self.vocab_size]  # Simulate tokenization
        
        # Check if we've seen this prefix before
        cache_key = "_".join(str(t) for t in self.conversation_history)
        if cache_key in self.kv_cache:
            # Reuse cached computation from previous turns
            cached_kv = self.kv_cache[cache_key]
            cached_cost_saved = 0.7 * len(self.conversation_history)  # 70% savings
        else:
            # First time seeing this conversation history
            cached_cost_saved = 0
            self.kv_cache[cache_key] = "simulated_cached_kv"
        
        # Generate response (only new tokens computed)
        sequence = []
        for _ in range(15):  # Shorter for dialogue
            logits = np.random.randn(self.vocab_size) * 2
            scaled_logits = logits / temperature
            probs = self._softmax(scaled_logits)
            
            # Sample from top-p
            top_k = 50
            top_indices = np.argsort(probs)[-top_k:]
            top_probs = probs[top_indices]
            top_probs = top_probs / np.sum(top_probs)
            
            next_token = np.random.choice(top_indices, p=top_probs)
            sequence.append(next_token)
            
            if next_token == 0:
                break
        
        # Update conversation history for next turn
        self.conversation_history.extend(sequence)
        
        result = self._decode_sequence(sequence, 
                                      f"Multi-turn Turn {turn_number} (BEST PRACTICE)")
        result += f" [Cache savings: {cached_cost_saved:.1f}% computation avoided]"
        return result
    
    def _decode_sequence(self
sections.label:
str) -> str:
        """Convert token IDs to human-readable text."""
        words = [self.cricket_vocab.get(t, f"tok{t}") for t in tokens]
        return f"{label}: {' '.join(words)}"
    
    def demonstrate_patterns(self):
        """Compare all patterns side by side."""
        print("=" * 80)
        print("ANTIPATTERN vs BEST PRACTICES")
        print("=" * 80)
        
        # Antipattern
        print("\n1. ANTIPATTERN - Greedy Decoding:")
        print("   Problem: Always picks argmax, produces repetitive, boring text")
        print("   " + self.generate_greedy(max_length=15))
        
        # Best Practice 1
        print("\n2. BEST PRACTICE - Temperature + Top-P:")
        print("   Benefit: Balanced diversity with safety bounds")
        for i in range(3):
            print("   " + self.generate_temperature_topk_sampling(max_length=15))
        
        # Best Practice 2
        print("\n3. BEST PRACTICE - Constrained (JSON):")
        print("   Benefit: Guarantees format compliance, zero chance of invalid output")
        print("   " + self.generate_constrained_json_stats(max_length=20))
        
        # Best Practice 3
        print("\n4. BEST PRACTICE - Multi-turn Cached:")
        print("   Benefit: 50-70% faster for dialogue, reuses prior computation")
        print("   " + self.generate_multiturned_with_cache("How's Rohit playing?", turn_number=1))
        print("   " + self.generate_multiturned_with_cache("Tell me more", turn_number=2))
        print("   " + self.generate_multiturned_with_cache("And the bowling?", turn_number=3))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    generator = CricketCommentaryGenerator()
    generator.demonstrate_patterns()

sections.content: Warning: The most common beginner mistake is confusing temperature with randomness when temperature actually affects the probability distribution's shape. A model with temperature=0.1 is not 'more random'—it's more deterministic, always picking the highest-probability token (approaching greedy behavior). Conversely, temperature=2.0 makes the distribution flatter, giving low-probability tokens higher chances—this is 'more random' in outcome but happens through a different mechanism. Another critical mistake is applying temperature directly to logits without understanding the resulting probability scale: temp_scaled_logits = logits / T followed by softmax, not logits * T. Using the wrong formula inverts the effect entirely. A third trap is forgetting that temperature affects all tokens equally; if a model's logits for vocabulary are [10, 9, 1], temperature just rescales them proportionally, never reordering them. To avoid these mistakes: (1) understand that temperature controls distribution flatness, not noise; (2) always use logits / T (division, not multiplication); (3) test temperature values empirically (0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0) and observe outputs before deploying; (4) remember that even temperature=2.0 cannot make a probability distribution completely uniform—only close to uniform.
Real-World Application

Advanced decoding strategies are fundamental to production LLM systems at scale. OpenAI's ChatGPT defaults to temperature=1.0 with nucleus sampling (p=0.95) to balance helpfulness with coherence, allowing slight diversity while preventing hallucinations. Google's production Gemini system uses constrained decoding to guarantee API-compatible JSON responses for structured data extraction tasks. Meta's Llama-based inference engine leverages prefix caching across 10,000+ concurrent requests, reusing KV-caches from identical conversation prefixes (e.g., 'Summarize' followed by identical documents) to reduce latency from 500ms to 150ms per response. Anthropic's Claude system implements custom decoding strategies per task: constitutional AI uses special decoding constraints to enforce safety guidelines at inference time rather than relying solely on training. In enterprise settings like OpenAI's API, temperature is exposed as a user parameter (0 to 2), with typical applications using 0 for deterministic tasks (code generation, data extraction) and 0.7-1.0 for creative tasks (writing, brainstorming). Cloud inference providers like Replicate and Together AI optimize multi-turn conversations through request batching combined with prefix caching, achieving 10-100x latency improvements for dialogue workloads. The financial impact is substantial: a reduction from 5 tokens/second to 15 tokens/second decoding throughput (via optimizations like speculative decoding) directly translates to serving 3x more users with the same hardware, or equivalently, reducing per-token inference costs by 66%. At scale, a 1% latency improvement across millions of daily queries can save hundreds of thousands in compute infrastructure costs monthly.

items: Temperature scales the logit values before softmax (divide by T), controlling whether the model picks argmax (low T≈0.1, deterministic) or explores uniformly (high T≈2.0, diverse); it does not add noise but reshapes probability distributions.

Top-k sampling restricts vocabulary to the k highest-probability tokens, while nucleus (top-p) sampling adaptively selects tokens until cumulative probability reaches p; both prevent the long-tail hallucination problem where extremely low-probability tokens are selected.

Beam search maintains b parallel hypotheses and explores non-greedily, avoiding local optima by deferring commitment to token choices; it produces superior results but requires b× computational cost compared to greedy sampling.

KV-cache optimization stores pre-computed attention key-value pairs for input tokens, reducing decoding from O(sequence_length²) to O(sequence_length) complexity and enabling latency reductions of 50-80% in multi-turn dialogue.
